did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Genetic Programming (GP), an AI/evolutionary computation method, to automatically extract global and local features from skin cancer images for classification. It proposes a GP-based approach (ARDGP) with adaptive region detection to classify skin cancer images, evaluating its performance on three skin cancer image classification tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research article' in the header. It presents original experimental work proposing a new genetic programming approach (ARDGP) for skin cancer image classification, including methodology, datasets (PH2binary, PH2multi, HAMmulti), results with statistical comparisons, and further analysis of parameter settings and convergence.

title: A genetic programming approach with adaptive region detection to skin cancer image classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a genetic programming approach with adaptive region detection (ARDGP) to automatically extract global and local features from skin cancer images for classification. The method addresses limitations in interpretability and feature extraction by introducing a novel function set and a region detection function to identify lesion areas. The performance of ARDGP was evaluated on three skin cancer image classification tasks using the PH2binary, PH2multi, and HAMmulti datasets. The approach was compared against two GP-based methods and six non-GP benchmark methods using balanced accuracy as the primary metric. Experimental results demonstrated that ARDGP achieved significantly better or similar performance in most cases compared to the benchmarks. The study concludes that ARDGP effectively utilizes region information and offers an interpretable, automated solution for assisting dermatologists in skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in PH2binary (malignant class), PH2multi, and HAMmulti (MEL class, 110 images).
Studied in PH2binary (merged with atypical nevi into one benign class, 80 images), PH2multi, and HAMmulti (NV class, 670 images).
Studied in PH2multi (80 images). In PH2binary, these were merged with benign images.
Studied in HAMmulti (110 images). Note: The paper lists 'BKL' in the HAMmulti description, which typically stands for Benign Keratosis.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (benign vs malignant, or specific lesion classes like melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'skin cancer image classification'. It evaluates the proposed ARDGP method on three classification tasks: PH2binary (binary classification: benign vs malignant), PH2multi (multi-class classification: melanoma, benign, atypical nevi), and HAMmulti (multi-class classification: AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC). The primary metric used is balanced accuracy, and the fitness function is based on classification accuracy (TP and FN counts per class).

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 200 skin cancer images covering three classes: melanoma, benign, and atypical nevi. It was divided into two classification tasks: PH2binary and PH2multi.

use.training: Used for training via five-fold cross-validation (four segments for training, one for testing in each fold).
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation (one segment for testing in each fold, repeated five times).
use.testing: Used for testing via five-fold cross-validation (one segment for testing in each fold, repeated five times).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of the proposed ARDGP method with benchmark methods (GP and non-GP) to determine if differences in classification accuracy were statistically significant.
To determine statistical significance in the Wilcoxon rank-sum test comparisons.
To evaluate the fitness value of GP individuals and estimate model performance by segmenting the training set into five equal parts, repeating the cycle five times with different test subsets.
To evaluate the fitness of ARDGP individuals and compare performance across methods, specifically to address bias introduced by imbalanced class distributions in medical datasets.

statistical_methods_used.method: Average balanced accuracy and Standard Deviation (Std)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the central tendency and variability of performance metrics after running experiments 30 times with distinct random seeds.

statistical_methods_used.method: 30 independent runs with distinct random seeds
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure the reliability and statistical robustness of the results by averaging performance metrics over multiple stochastic executions.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance between the proposed ARDGP method and the benchmark methods.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance between the proposed ARDGP method and the benchmark methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Wilcoxon rank-sum test with a 5% significance level was employed to compare the performance of ARDGP with benchmark methods. The '+' and '-' symbols indicate whether the performance of ARDGP is significantly better or worse than the compared method, while the '=' symbol signifies similar performance to the benchmark method.

conclusions: The proposed Adaptive Region Detection Genetic Programming (ARDGP) approach effectively addresses limitations in existing skin cancer image classification methods, such as poor interpretability, reliance on domain knowledge for feature extraction, and neglect of lesion area information.

ARDGP automatically and flexibly extracts effective global and local features from skin images by utilizing a novel function set and a region detection function to select lesion areas, providing a comprehensive description of skin images.

ARDGP achieves significantly better or similar performance compared to three GP-based methods (FLGP, FLGP_SCD, FGP) and six non-GP benchmark methods across three classification tasks (PH2binary, PH2multi, HAMmulti).
The inclusion of region detection functions allows ARDGP to utilize local information of the lesion area, which contributes to its superior or comparable performance against benchmarks.

ARDGP offers an interpretable solution for skin cancer diagnosis, as the evolved GP trees can be visualized to show the features and regions used, addressing the 'black-box' nature of many deep learning models.
Parameter analysis indicates that a population size of 100 and a maximum of 50 generations provide an optimal trade-off between test accuracy, stability, and computational cost (training time).

ARDGP demonstrates good convergence ability and robustness, as validated by experiments run 30 times with distinct random seeds and statistical significance testing (Wilcoxon rank-sum test at 5% level).
important_remarks: The study relies on small, curated subsets of public datasets (PH2 and a 10% sample of HAM10000) rather than the full HAM10000 dataset, which limits the assessment of performance on large-scale, real-world data.
The HAMmulti dataset is significantly imbalanced (e.g., 670 NV vs. 12 DF images), although the authors address this by using balanced accuracy as the primary metric.
The PH2binary task merges 'atypical nevi' with 'benign' lesions, which may obscure diagnostic challenges specific to distinguishing atypical nevi from benign nevi.
The proposed ARDGP method was evaluated using only three classifiers (KNN, RF, SVM) for the final classification layer; other potential classifiers were not tested.
The method was tested on only three classification tasks (PH2binary, PH2multi, HAMmulti); external validation on other independent skin cancer datasets is not reported.

The approach uses a linear SVM as the default classifier, but the paper notes that performance varies depending on the classifier used (e.g., ARDGP with SVM vs. NB), suggesting classifier choice impacts results.
The paper does not report computational complexity or inference time for the ARDGP method, only training time for parameter analysis, which is a gap for deployment considerations.

The study focuses on binary and multi-class classification of specific lesion types; it does not address multi-label classification or uncertainty quantification, which are relevant for clinical deployment.
